terms.definition: Algorithmic design decision to spend more of one resource (memory or compute) to save the other.
A bounded store of previously computed results keyed by their inputs; trades memory for time.
Rule deciding which cache entries to discard when capacity is reached (LRU, LFU, FIFO, TTL).
Lifetime after which a cached value is considered stale and must be recomputed.
In LLM inference, the cached K and V projections per token per layer that let decoding skip the O(n²) recompute.
Reducing model weight precision (fp16 → int8/int4) to save memory and often compute, at the cost of some quality.
Caching results of expensive function calls; the most common 'pay memory, save time' move at code level.
Processing data as it arrives, holding O(1) (or O(buffer)) memory instead of O(n).
Approximate or exact data structure (HNSW, IVF, PQ) accelerating nearest-neighbor lookup at the cost of build time and memory.
